data.title: Can prediction markets win the race for perpetual contracts?
data.description:
Over the past year, we've spent considerable time reporting on perpetual futures (PERS) trading platforms. Their rapid rise is undeniable. Perpetual futures allow participants to price events shortly after they occur, offering high leverage and ample liquidity around the clock. Existing exchanges have never offered this service due to limitations in trading hours and days. An 11-member team has built Hyperliquid into the fastest-growing cryptocurrency exchange with nearly $1 billion in annual revenue thanks to this 24/7 trading concept.

In 2025, perpetual futures trading volume averaged seven times that of spot trading. This seemed like a reliable path to building a sustainable business. So, inevitably, others followed suit.

Last week, two major prediction markets, Polymarket and Kalshi, announced the launch of perpetual futures and cryptocurrency trading within hours of each other. And just a few months ago, Hyperliquid also announced the launch of event contracts.

The convergence of perpetual contracts and prediction market platforms seems like a natural progression. Everyone wants to be an all-encompassing exchange, offering a one-stop shop for attention, capital, and leverage. Three weeks ago, Saurabh wrote in a report on X that Hyperliquid's foray into prediction markets would help the exchange dominate the financial arena. But does the reverse hold true? Will Polymarket and Kalshi's moves yield similar returns? Today, I'll tell you all about it. Why Perpetual Contracts Are Important for Prediction Markets Prediction markets suffer from stickiness. They are typically cyclical, with trading volumes reaching all-time highs when there are major events to bet on—like during US presidential elections, the Super Bowl season, or Federal Open Market Committee meetings. During the US presidential election in November 2024, Polymarket's monthly active users peaked at 321,500. Three weeks later, this number dropped by 25% to 245,000. However, the number of monthly users fluctuates due to seasonal factors. In January 2025, Polymarket's user base peaked at 500,000, before falling below 200,000 in September. This reflects Polymarket's user retention rate. Dune's user base data shows that since 2024, only 8% to 11% of users have remained active in the market a year after joining. Approximately 75% of users churn within 90 days. Users may return to participate in events, but this doesn't necessarily translate to a sense of engagement with the platform.

But this is only part of the problem.

Prediction markets also freeze funds until the problem is resolved. Perpetual contracts, on the other hand, update event prices every second, thus attracting attention for a longer period and building continuous user interaction. This is also more advantageous for prediction markets because traders have larger trading volumes and higher commission revenue.

In 2025, the nominal trading volume of malicious traders exceeded $60 trillion, while the nominal trading volume of precious metals traders was $28 billion.

In 2025, the nominal trading volume of malicious traders exceeded $60 trillion, while the nominal trading volume of precious metals traders was $28 billion.

Therefore, this expansion into adjacent domains of prediction markets (PMs) becomes a natural evolution. Platforms that cater to certain speculative needs often expand their business into other areas. They either develop the relevant functionality themselves or acquire other platforms that possess that functionality. We have witnessed this many times: Robinhood expanded from the stock market to the options market, the cryptocurrency market, and finally into prediction markets (PMs). Coinbase acquired Deribit for a record $2.9 billion, entering the derivatives trading space. Binance also expanded from offering spot trading to futures trading, eventually creating its own native blockchain. We often see this in traditional sectors. A company expands its services, hoping to cross-sell new products to the same group of customers. This serves two purposes: first, to increase average revenue per user (ARPU), and second, to diversify reliance on multiple revenue streams, thereby enhancing the company's resilience to market cycle fluctuations. In the early 1970s, commodity futures revenue at the Chicago Board of Trade (CBOT) was declining. So, they used a former 4,000-square-foot smoking lounge at their parent company, CBOT, to create the Chicago Board Options Exchange (now Cboe). Because both required shared infrastructure, they were able to work together: risk management, clearing, and a network of professionals knowledgeable in derivatives pricing. However, there's a huge gap between wanting to operate a perpetual contract trading platform and actually having the ability to implement it. Operating a perpetual trading platform involves many aspects. Let's start with liquidity. The Hyperliquid platform processes over 200,000 orders per second through a fully on-chain order book. This exchange settles over $6-7 billion in trading volume daily, using a two-sided market-making model. Insufficient liquidity can lead to extreme volatility, excessive bid-ask spreads, and high slippage, making it easier for whales to manipulate prices. Secondly, there's the risk engine—the core of any derivatives platform. It tracks every trade and checks the margin requirements for each order. In October 2025, the cryptocurrency market lost $19 billion, and the Hyperliquid platform processed billions of dollars in liquidations without interrupting service. Additionally, there's the funding rate mechanism, which links a trader's price to the spot price of the underlying asset. This mechanism operates continuously by settling small amounts between long and short positions every few hours. Building the entire technology stack isn't the main problem; I believe prediction markets can do that. The bigger problem lies in stress testing this technology stack. Hyperliquid built all of these systems and stress-tested them in real-world scenarios, such as a 10/10 cryptocurrency liquidation event and the Iraq War. Afterward, once the entire system was ready, it launched event contracts via HIP-4. Kalshi and Polymarket, on the other hand, are trying to do the opposite. They operate successful prediction markets that don't require any of the systems mentioned above. Now, they not only have to compete with the highly successful Hyperliquid, but also with a system that hasn't been stress-tested and can't handle the high-frequency activity of perpetual trading, thus vying for market share. For prediction markets, there are many headwinds that make expanding to perpetual trading more difficult than the other way around. Hedging Synergies On the Hyperliquid platform, the Risk Engine monitors all your positions across all trading instruments, spot, and upcoming event contracts. Saurabh explains this in his HIP-4 report. It looks at all your positions, indiscriminately. Ultimately, the leverage you use and the margin you hold as cross-collateral determine when you will be forced to liquidate. The combination of your positions in spot, futures, prediction markets, or any other market determines how much margin you need to hold. But Saurabh, aren't other blockchains like Ethereum or Solana also composable? Absolutely. On a universal chain, each application runs its own risk engine within its own smart contract. They cannot atomically see each other's state. Therefore, Kamino cannot know what's happening on Pacifica. Aave cannot know what's happening on Lighter. All applications are smart contracts on their own chains. Each application or smart contract has an independent risk engine, and to make them aware of each other—that is, to create a universal risk engine—requires massive collaboration. This universal risk engine solves a core funding problem by optimizing the same funds across multiple trades made by a trader on a trading platform. Let's say a trader is going long ETH on the Hyperliquid platform with 5x leverage. She's worried about the Federal Reserve's interest rate decision next week, so she buys a "Fed Keeps Interest Rates Unchanged" outcome contract at $0.65. Because the same risk engine is used, both positions are held in the same margin account. If the Federal Reserve unexpectedly cuts interest rates and the price of ETH rises, her long position profits, while the loss in the outcome contract is only her initial investment. If the Federal Reserve keeps interest rates unchanged, the outcome contract will pay out gains, partially offsetting the loss on her long position. This is why prediction market platforms or hedging trading venues cannot simply be add-ons. This hedging possibility is precisely the value of HIP-4 on the Hyperliquid platform. Ordinary traders on the platform view prediction markets as insurance against the reversal of their existing hedging positions. Currently, collateral on the Polymarket and Kalshi platforms will be locked until the event is resolved. Therefore, unless they provide a unified risk engine between their real-money trading and prediction market trading venues, they will lose a key factor that keeps traders on the platform. Currently, neither of these platforms has announced a cross-margin system between their prediction market trading and real-money trading venues. The segmentation of prediction markets and the average trader profile have further fueled concerns about their ability to replicate their success in real-money trading. Over 80% of Kalshi's total monthly trading volume comes from sports betting. Polymarket's figure is projected to exceed 40% by 2025. So how can a sustainable pricing mechanism be built around these sporting events for paid trading platforms? This would exclude a significant portion of traders from paid trading. Furthermore, Kalshi's average traders are retail investors with no prior cryptocurrency experience, funding their prediction market accounts via ACH transfers from bank accounts. Therefore, even assuming the theoretical possibility of cross-margining on the Kalshi platform, I doubt whether these traders possess the expertise required to double down on their investments and utilize perpetual contracts as a hedging strategy. What approaches might be effective for prediction markets? If Kalshi and Polymarket announce cross-margining, I believe there's one scenario where these bets would work. Their institutional partnerships with major brokers and clearinghouses could facilitate high-value, high-frequency trading activity in event contracts and perpetual futures. This would allow institutional trading desks to view prediction markets as part of a broader risk management toolkit. Both Kalshi and Polymarket have partnerships that allow them to reach institutional clients. Kalshi's partnerships with FIS and Tradeweb data, and Polymarket's deal with the Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), could help retain institutional clients who value using perpetual contracts to hedge their prediction market positions on the same platform. This remains a distant goal, requiring many factors in the prediction market to move in a favorable direction. They need to build stress-tested infrastructure, forge partnerships, and demonstrate to customers that their platform can help optimize capital allocation. But these are essential for their survival in this fiercely competitive market. With distribution channels already dominated by Hyperliquid, they have no choice but to seek maximum opportunities elsewhere.

data.title: Robots Land in Vietnam: Is AI the Next Big Thing in Southeast Asia?
data.description:
While everyone is still focused on Indonesian e-commerce, Thai tourism, and Singaporean finance, a group of Chinese AI entrepreneurs have quietly turned their attention to—Vietnam. Today's Vietnam is strikingly similar to China five years ago—high GDP growth, a massive influx of foreign investment, and a labor dividend that, while still present, has reached a turning point. The difference is that this time the story isn't about "moving factories in," but about "making factories smarter." From the robot baristas at Hanoi's "Coffee Shop of the Future" to the humanoid robots soon to be deployed at the Foxconn factory in Bac Ninh province, Vietnam's robot revolution has quietly begun. I. Why Vietnam? In 2025, Vietnam's GDP growth rate reached 8.02%, ranking among the top in Asia, and its per capita GDP exceeded US$5,000, successfully entering the ranks of middle-to-high-income countries. In the first quarter of 2026, the GDP growth rate was still as high as 7.83%, and registered FDI reached US$15.2 billion, a year-on-year increase of 42.9%, of which manufacturing accounted for more than 70% of FDI. Vietnam's population has exceeded 100 million, with young workers accounting for more than 60% and an average age of less than 33. But a problem has arisen—more and more factories are being built, but there are not enough young people. A contradiction of mixed feelings is forming: on the one hand, the manufacturing industry is growing larger and larger, and foreign investment is pouring in like crazy; on the other hand, labor costs are rising continuously, and young people are unwilling to "work in factories to tighten screws." This gap between "strong demand" and "insufficient supply" is precisely the fertile ground for the most natural "essential need" for robots. The Vietnamese government has also recognized this. By the end of 2025, Vietnam officially listed Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs) as one of the six priority strategic technology products, paving the way for the robotics industry at the national strategic level. II. What businesses can robots do in Vietnam? ① Warehousing and Logistics Robots – The Most Certain "Billion-Dollar Track" Vietnamese e-commerce is booming. The e-commerce market size reached $15.3 billion in 2025, with parcel volume continuing to climb at an annual growth rate of nearly 30%. However, a key statistic reveals a huge market gap: Vietnam's warehouse automation penetration rate is only 2-3%, while the global average is close to 10%. This means there is still 3-5 times the potential for growth. AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots), as core equipment for flexible automation, are moving from the "proof-of-concept" stage to the "large-scale deployment" stage. Market research institutions predict that Vietnam's warehouse automation market will continue to maintain double-digit growth. Leading players have already established successful models. Viettel Post's automated sorting center has achieved a daily processing capacity of 1.4 million parcels—the technical feasibility has been proven; the remaining task is simply to scale up replication. ② Industrial Quality Inspection and Intelligent Manufacturing – AI is Transforming Production Lines The Vietnamese manufacturing AI market is projected to grow from $1.15 billion in 2025 to $4.8 billion in 2031, representing a CAGR of 26.6%. This is not just empty talk. In March 2026, Maihe Robotics exported a complete automotive welding production line to Vietnam, marking the "overall export" of Chinese intelligent equipment to Vietnamese factories. Hongban Technology invested $110 million in Ninh Binh province to build an intelligent manufacturing base, fully introducing world-class automated production lines. Yadea completed its first phase of intelligent factory construction in Bac Ninh province, Vietnam, with an investment exceeding $100 million and an annual production capacity of one million electric vehicles. Samsung, Foxconn, Luxshare Precision, and other giants have already heavily invested in northern Vietnam. These high-standard factories are forcing the entire upstream supply chain to upgrade towards automation—quality inspection robots, collaborative robots, and AGVs are becoming standard equipment in foreign-invested factories. Service and Humanoid Robots—From "Novelty" to "Essential Need" Hanoi's "Coffee Shop of the Future" is already a hit—four robots act as baristas, waiters, calligraphers, and photographers, working 6-8 hours a day without interruption, with young consumers lining up to experience it. In Ho Chi Minh City, robots are already helping to deliver documents in government service centers, acting as guides at exhibitions, and mixing drinks at hotel bars. An even stronger signal comes from Foxconn. In April 2026, a Foxconn subsidiary submitted an environmental impact assessment report to the Vietnamese government, planning to produce humanoid industrial robots at its factory in Bac Ninh province, with official production commencing in November 2026. The global manufacturing giant is building a "humanoid robot production line" in Vietnam—the significance of this signal is self-evident. Vietnam is leveraging its "latecomer advantage," skipping the purely mechanical stage and directly entering the AI-driven intelligent robot market. This path is progressing faster than expected. III. Who is quietly making these moves? Chinese companies have already established a presence in Vietnam. Aishida announced a 150 million RMB investment in Vietnam to build production lines for cookware, small appliances, and industrial robots, stating that this "is beneficial for the company's rapid strategic deployment of industrial robots in Vietnam and the Southeast Asian market." Moji Robotics signed a strategic cooperation agreement with Vietnam's Geleximco Group to comprehensively promote robot R&D, manufacturing, and application in Vietnam. Sophis Intelligent showcased its flexible robot technology at the Vietnam Industrial Exhibition, signing a Vietnamese distributor agreement on-site. More than 50 Vietnamese heavy industry companies have expressed their need for transformation.

· Vietnam's local strength: More than just Chinese players

· VinRobotics & VinMotionVinRobotics, a subsidiary of Vingroup, Vietnam's largest private enterprise, was established at the end of 2024 and produced a robot prototype from scratch within seven months.

【Vietnam's local strength: More than just Chinese players】

If Chinese companies are the "beachhead raiders," then Vietnamese local giants have begun to "fight on their home turf," and they are acting swiftly and with great determination.

· VinRobotics & VinMotion: A subsidiary of Vingroup, Vietnam's largest private enterprise, it was only established at the end of 2024 and produced a robot prototype from scratch within seven months.

The products are already in use at the VinFast factory and are working closely with Qualcomm, directly targeting the top-tier international market. VinDynamics: Also part of Vingroup, focusing on humanoid robot R&D, it recently partnered with German parts giant Schaeffler to accelerate its global expansion. CT Group: A Vietnamese conglomerate with a market capitalization of 250 billion VND, it previously secured an order to export 5,000 drones to South Korea, pursuing a dual-track strategy of drones and robots. Menas Group: It has deployed Unitree Robotics' MEME and NANA robot dogs in high-end shopping malls in Vietnam, using service robots to optimize customer experience; the speed of commercialization is astonishing. Furthermore, the American robotics company AMC Robotics has also established a factory in Ho Chi Minh City in January 2026 to produce industrial quadruped robots, making Vietnam its Asian production hub. International giants are simultaneously increasing their investment. Qualcomm has just announced the 28 shortlisted startups for its 2026 Vietnam Innovation Challenge, with AI and robotics being the core tracks. Ho Chi Minh City has established an initial 500 billion VND (approximately US$20 million) technology venture capital fund, specifically focusing on robotics and automation. Capital and industry are accelerating simultaneously. From Chinese entrepreneurs to Vietnamese giants, and then to international supply chains—three forces are simultaneously investing in Vietnam. This is not a bubble, but a structural industrial transfer. IV. Risks? Of course there are. No market is without its challenges. Vietnam also faces several hurdles: • Incomplete industrial chain: core components rely on imports, and the local supply chain is not yet mature. High after-sales costs: Local robot and automation engineers are scarce, requiring maintenance teams to be built from scratch. • Tiered willingness to pay: Large clients (such as Samsung and Foxconn) have ample budgets, but small and medium-sized factories are accustomed to a "try before you buy" approach. • Power bottleneck: Vietnam's peak electricity consumption in 2025 is projected to increase by 11.1% year-on-year, and fluctuations in energy supply may affect the operation of automated equipment. However, these challenges are precisely the barriers to entry—whoever establishes a successful localized service system first builds a competitive advantage. V. Conclusion: How Long is the Window of Opportunity? If the story of Vietnam over the past 10 years was "from an agricultural country to the world's factory," then the story of the next 10 years will undoubtedly be "factories transforming themselves into smart factories." Robots are no longer a question of "whether or not to come," but rather "whether arriving late will lead to missed opportunities." When AI transforms from a concept into a machine capable of working continuously for 16 hours in a humid and hot workshop—the gold rush map of Southeast Asia will be completely redrawn. The window is still there, but it won't stay open forever.

data.title: Why do investors from all over the world flock to Buffett's Omaha meeting?
data.description:
With the Omaha Shareholders Meeting on May 2, 2026, drawing ever closer, my friend Leo (a close friend who translates Warren Buffett's letter to shareholders with Lao Wang) is both researching travel plans and answering fans' questions: "Why is Berkshire Hathaway's shareholders meeting so special? It lasts all day, with shopping and airplane viewing—it's like a carnival!"

That's certainly a good question. Today's shareholders meeting format isn't just something casually arranged. It actually reflects Buffett's unique ideas about corporate governance, shareholder relations, and capital culture.

Let's travel back in time and see how this gentleman, step by step, transformed a dry, legally necessary procedure into "the Woodstock of the capitalists."

First Step

Escaping Mondays: When "Shareholders' Choice" Wins Over the Chairman

Now, shareholder meetings are fixed on Saturdays, which seems natural. But initially, this was even against Buffett's personal wishes.

In 2002, the new Qwest Center in Omaha was completed, with ample parking, and Buffett, for the first time, gave shareholders the choice of meeting date. He stated explicitly: "I want to hold it on Mondays." The reason was practical: weekdays were more convenient for him and his team.

However, shareholder democracy gave a completely different answer.

According to the 2003 letter, "We won on Saturday by a 2:1 vote." Buffett, true to his word, added humorously, "At Berkshire, shareholder democracy will take some time to return." This had a significant impact. Holding meetings on Saturdays was essentially for the convenience of shareholders, not management. It allowed commuters to attend and greatly alleviated traffic and parking headaches (a point Buffett mentioned many times later). This established the tone: the shareholders' meeting was first and foremost a holiday for shareholders, and only secondarily a formality for the company.

Step Two

Disrupt the Agenda, Relegate the "Boring Parts"

An even more drastic change came in 2004. Buffett performed a major overhaul on the traditional shareholder meeting process.

Previously, formal shareholder proposals and voting took center stage. But Buffett discovered that a few proposers "raised topics that were of little interest to most of the audience, wasting a lot of time—they must have enjoyed speaking to a roughly 19,500-strong audience." (2004 Letter to Shareholders)

His solution was simple, brutal, and practical: reverse the order of the agenda.

Since 2004, the process has changed: the doors open at 7:00 AM, a movie starts at 8:30 AM, and a Q&A session that lasts for several hours, beginning at 9:30 AM and continuing until 3:00 PM. Only then does the formal meeting take place briefly. Buffett explained, "This way, shareholders who want to attend the full meeting can stay, and those who aren't interested can leave early, or better yet, go shopping." The essence of this change is two words: **respect**. Respecting the value of shareholders' time. The most informative Q&A sessions are placed during prime time, while legally required formalities are postponed. It assumes that shareholders are there to learn and exchange ideas, not to fulfill tedious obligations. Meanwhile, the phrase "or better yet, go shopping" unabashedly treated shareholders as customers and business partners, pioneering a new approach that blended "exhibitions" and "conferences." The third step: Embracing technology and using live streaming to resolve the double awkwardness. In 2015, Buffett accomplished another major feat: broadcasting the annual shareholders' meeting globally for the first time. His reasoning in the letter was so candid it was almost laughable: "There's really no other way: it might reduce the number of people coming. Last year, over 40,000 people came, the pressure is immense." Facing reality: "The second reason is even more important. Charlie is 92, and I'm 85… Shareholders shouldn't have to travel all the way to Omaha just to see how we look and sound. (Please be kind with your comments, after all, even at our best, we weren't exactly pleasing to the eye.)" This candor is invaluable. It not only solved the problem of insufficient seating but also, in a humorous and direct way, responded to everyone's whispers about "whether the old men are still up to par." The online livestream didn't diminish the value of the in-person event; instead, it served as a global invitation, expanding Berkshire Hathaway's community culture and making the experience of physically attending Omaha even cooler.

Step Four

Activating the Showroom, Bringing the Shopping Center into the Convention Center

From initially urging attendees to "bring your money" at the Nebraska Furniture Market to later creating a nearly 200,000-square-foot showroom in the convention center, shopping has always been an indispensable part of the shareholders' meeting. This isn't just simple sales; Buffett understands human nature perfectly, achieving three goals at once: Creating a hands-on experience: Letting shareholders see and touch Berkshire Hathaway's products (from See's Candies to Clayton mobile homes) transforms the abstract "investment portfolio" into a concrete, engaging consumer experience. Shareholders instantly become customers, boosting their sense of connection. Promoting subsidiaries: This is the perfect stage for subsidiaries to showcase their strength to their most important and friendly customers. CEOs can chat directly with shareholders. Providing an interesting place to go: As mentioned earlier, it provides a fantastic place for shareholders who aren't interested in formal processes. The whole event is therefore very flexible, ensuring everyone can have fun. The shareholders' meeting thus became a closed-loop ecosystem: morning Q&A sessions absorbed ideas, afternoon shopping released energy, and shareholders seamlessly switched between the roles of "boss," "student," and "shopaholic." The meeting was like a closed loop: shareholders absorbed ideas through Q&A in the morning and released energy through shopping in the afternoon, seamlessly switching between roles as "boss," "student," and "shopaholic." Step Five: Optimizing Q&A, from "Seating Frenzy" to "Fair Lottery" With more and more people attending, figuring out how to ask questions became a challenge. There were even instances of "a 100-meter sprint" as the meeting opened at 7 am, with everyone frantically scrambling for microphone positions. In 2008, Buffett had to be honest: "From a security perspective, this won't work, and we don't think that those who are quick enough should have the right to ask questions." So, he introduced a lottery system. Starting in 2009, at 8:15 AM on the day of the meeting, a lottery was held in front of each microphone to determine who would ask a question. At the same time, the sources of questions became more diverse, with some coming from shareholders selected in the audience and others from submissions screened by journalists, ensuring that the questions were both informative and varied. This change reflects Buffett's commitment to "fairness" and "quality." It avoided a scramble for attention, gave every attending shareholder an equal opportunity, and made the Q&A session more substantial and engaging. Looking at these changes together, Buffett's design logic couldn't be clearer: Shareholders are the main players: prioritize comfort and efficiency. Extreme pragmatism: avoid formalism and dare to overturn old rules for truly valuable things. Speak your mind: don't shy away from problems (such as age or large numbers), use humor and practical actions to alleviate concerns. Build an ecosystem: upgrade meetings into super parties that integrate ideas, business, and culture. What he orchestrated wasn't just a meeting, but a large, tangible party for a highly trusting, interactive, and engaging shareholder family. Here, shareholders didn't feel aloof respect, but rather a warm sense of belonging.

data.title: The farce of a 20-year-old internet celebrity becoming a father has turned a multi-million dollar bet on Polymarket into a joke.
data.description:
Author: Mahe, Foresight News

On Polymarket, everything is bet. On May 1st, the prediction market surrounding "whether Clavicular will become a father in 2026" had accumulated over $14 million in trading volume, with approximately $4.5 million traded in the past 24 hours.

The screenshot shows that the market was created on April 20, 2026. This isn't ordinary sports or election betting; it's an extreme bet surrounding the personal life of a 20-year-old Kick anchor—whether he will make a "credible" announcement of becoming a father by December 31, 2026.

... The rules are clearly stated: only credible statements from Clavicular himself or his representatives are valid; jokes or unreliable content are invalid; the announcement date is the determining factor, regardless of the child's actual birth date. However, the protagonist merely announced his girlfriend's pregnancy during a live stream, and Polymarket, after multiple rounds of controversy and voting, ruled it "Yes." This quickly sparked widespread online discussion and debate, primarily concerning two aspects: first, the sensitivity of the topic itself—is turning a social media influencer's "birth plan" into a multi-million dollar bet overstepping boundaries? Second, the fairness of the oracle settlement mechanism. The UMA decentralized oracle repeatedly proposed "Yes," leading to controversial voting, with the final token-weighted result overwhelming the majority of individual voters, resulting in a deluge of accusations of "manipulation" in the comments section. When large investors are both bettors and voters, can the truth still prevail?

20-year-old internet celebrity who once sparked huge controversy

Clavicular's real name is Braden Eric Peters. At only 20 years old, he grew up in an ordinary middle-class family; his father was a businessman, and his mother was a bodybuilding enthusiast. He rose to fame through live streaming on the Kick platform and short videos on TikTok.

Clavicular's tagline is "looksmaxxing"—an extreme subculture of physical enhancement that emphasizes "hardcore enhancement" of attractiveness through drugs, surgery, and lifestyle choices.

Clavicular's tagline is "looksmaxxing"—a subculture of extreme physical enhancement that emphasizes "hardcore enhancement" of attractiveness through drugs, surgery, and lifestyle choices.

He began injecting testosterone and other controlled substances at age 14, publicly discussing the anorexic effects of bone marrow removal, bimaxillary osteotomy, liposuction injections, steroids, peptides, and methamphetamine, even calling himself a "lab guinea pig" in the looksmaxxing community. His username, "Clavicular," is derived from his collarbone width—he claimed his shoulders were 19.5 inches wide, pursuing a golden ratio face and physique. His content primarily targeted young male viewers, promoting the idea that "appearance equals power." From 2025 onwards, his exposure increased exponentially. He walked the runway at New York Fashion Week, gave interviews to mainstream media outlets such as the New York Times and GQ, but also frequently became embroiled in scandals: in March 2026, he was arrested for assault; in mid-April, he allegedly overdosed on drugs during a live stream and collapsed, later being hospitalized. Several of his YouTube channels were permanently banned for violations (promoting controlled substances, harmful content targeting teenagers). In April 2026, he proposed a "concubine-selection" fertility plan: during a live stream, he announced a "dating show" similar to a beauty pageant, recruiting 500 women to participate, with the winner becoming the mother of his child. He publicly stated, "I must have a child, I want a child today," and "I will become a father by the end of next year," and even shared his "fertility stack." These remarks quickly sparked a strong backlash on social media, accused of objectifying women. It was against this backdrop that Polymarket launched on April 20th, with traders generally believing he "would definitely announce it." Shortly after the market's launch, Clavicular's live stream again generated buzz. Recently, during a live stream, he emotionally claimed that he was pregnant with a child from a girlfriend he claimed to be 18 years old (whom he had only known for 10 days), breaking down in tears and emotionally. This clip quickly went viral, with related Kick clips garnering tens of millions of views. However, the facts were swiftly reversed. Multiple investigations revealed that the girlfriend had broken up with him and was not pregnant; the entire incident lacked any medical documentation, doctor's statement, or official announcement, and biologically, confirming and publicly announcing a pregnancy within 10 days is highly improbable. Clavicular himself did not subsequently issue a formal, credible statement, and the entire process resembled his usual chaotic live stream style. Despite this, Polymarket Yes's share increased instead of decreasing. Trading volume surged to $4.5 million in a single day during the controversy, with some large investors placing heavy bets. UMA Oracle Voting Manipulated Again According to Polymarket rules and the UMA oracle mechanism, settlement is phased: proposers first submit Yes/No, which can be challenged within the dispute window. If it enters the DVM (Data Verification Mechanism), UMA token holders vote. A minimum of 65% approval and 5 million UMA tokens from participants are required for approval. This market experienced two cycles of "proposed Yes → dispute," ultimately settling on Yes. According to the comments section below the Polymarket market, the controversy lies in the voting result: almost every long-time Polymarket user believes it shouldn't be considered Yes because it's premature and doesn't meet the judgment rules. Even some Yes holders share this opinion. However, in the token-weighted vote, Yes won with 67%. Key figures included UMA Rocks—the largest voting group of Polymarket traders who also held market positions. Polymarket player Domahhhh commented that member "Scout" was one of the largest Yes holders and a key member of UMA Rocks. Scout had been banned from Polymarket's Discord for suspected criminal activity before this incident. Scout then pushed UMA Rocks to officially vote Yes. The second-largest voter (a Risk Labs employee) immediately switched to Yes after the UMA Rocks vote. This was for self-preservation; if you voted against UMA, you would lose money. In the first round of voting, Yes led by a narrow margin because the vast majority of token holders voted Too Early, but UMA Rocks, along with a few large whales, voted for Yes. Due to UMA's mechanism—which encourages the most popular answer rather than the truth—any option that led in the previous round is highly likely to ultimately win. The vote now overwhelmingly favors Yes. Currently, Scout has been removed from UMA Rocks. Polymarket has not yet responded to this event, and players who bet on NO have already lost their chips.

data.title: Structural Cleansing in the Crypto Industry Amid Geopolitical Conflicts and Liquidity Retreat
data.description:
Preface

In the first quarter of 2026, global financial markets entered the most severe risk repricing cycle since 2022. The continued escalation of geopolitical conflicts, the full-scale return of trade protectionism, and the repeated swings in monetary policies of major economies collectively created a highly uncertain macroeconomic environment with low liquidity preferences. The crypto market suffered a systemic sell-off during this quarter, with Bitcoin plummeting from approximately $93,000 at the beginning of the year to the $63,000 range, a maximum drawdown of over 38%; the altcoin market was even more devastated, with many tokens falling 60%–80% from their cycle highs.

This deterioration in the market environment was not accidental, but rather the result of multiple structural pressures. On the one hand, the Trump administration's aggressive tariff policies and the US-Iran military conflict pushed geopolitical risks to new highs, putting pressure on risk assets across the board; on the other hand, the crypto market's own narrative fatigue and the fragility of its liquidity structure were fully exposed during the downward trend.

Bitcoin's narrative as digital gold failed to deliver on its safe-haven function in a wartime environment, instead exhibiting itself as a high-beta risk asset; altcoins, lacking new capital and fundamental support, have fallen into a continuous value regression. Meanwhile, the rapid evolution of AI is profoundly reshaping the intersection of technology and finance. The continuous iteration of large-scale model capabilities, the explosive growth of open-source AI agent frameworks, and the initial integration of AI with crypto payment scenarios provide noteworthy new variables for the industry's medium- to long-term development. The following analysis will dissect the core variables of the first quarter from four dimensions: the macro environment, Bitcoin and capital structure, AI development trends, and the differentiation of the altcoin market, and provide ArkStream Capital's framework judgment on future trends. Macroeconomic Environment: Geopolitical Conflicts and Trade Wars Constitute Systemic Pressure The fragmentation of the global trade system is accelerating. The primary analytical framework for understanding first-quarter policy actions is the US midterm elections in November 2026. The Republican Party's current slim majority in both the House and Senate faces a severe challenge; historically, the ruling party has almost inevitably lost seats in midterm elections. Under this pressure, the Trump administration's series of actions in the first quarter—including tough tariffs, the Greenland sovereignty narrative, and military strikes against Iran—essentially serve the same political logic: consolidating the Republican base through aggressive foreign and economic policies and creating a narrative of a "strong president" in an election year. This means that, at least before the November vote, the unpredictability and aggressiveness of policies are unlikely to cool down substantially, and the political uncertainty premium faced by risk assets will persist throughout the year. Driven by this political logic, the global trade order experienced its most severe shock since the establishment of the WTO in the first quarter. In mid-January, the Trump administration announced tiered tariffs of 10%–25% on eight countries: Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, the United Kingdom, the Netherlands, and Finland, using the Greenland sovereignty dispute as leverage. At the end of January, a bill imposing 500% punitive tariffs on countries purchasing Russian oil (directly targeting BRICS countries such as China, India, and Brazil) gained bipartisan support and entered the voting process. On February 20, after the U.S. Supreme Court rejected the legal basis for tariffs under the International Economic Partnership Agreement (IEEPA), Trump immediately invoked Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974 to reimpose a 10% global provisional tariff (valid for 150 days). The repeated escalations and changes in tariff policies impact the market from three dimensions: On the cost side, import tariffs directly drive up commodity prices and inflation expectations, limiting the Federal Reserve's room for interest rate cuts; on the supply chain side, retaliatory measures from trading partners exacerbate global supply chain uncertainty, suppressing companies' willingness to invest in capital; and on the risk premium side, the unpredictability of the policies themselves becomes the biggest pricing challenge, systematically increasing the implied volatility of various risky assets. ArkStream Capital believes that the latter two effects are far more difficult to quantify and fully price in the market than the tariff rates themselves.

The essential difference between this round of trade conflict and previous rounds of tariff negotiations lies in the fact that the conflict has expanded from bilateral to multilateral, and the repeated changes in the legal basis indicate that policy-making itself has deviated from the institutionalized track and entered a new stage of "immediate administrative power." The suppressive effect on risky assets far exceeds the direct economic impact of the tariff rate itself.

The Tail Risks of the US-Iran Military Conflict On February 28th, the US and Israel launched a coordinated military strike against Iran, causing oil prices to jump within hours and global financial markets to enter risk-averse mode. With traditional markets closed for the weekend, the cryptocurrency market, as the only major asset class trading 24/7, bore the brunt of the risk-averse selling pressure. Bitcoin fell from $65,500 to $63,000 within an hour, and over $515 million in leveraged positions were liquidated. This event once again confirms the fact that, under the current institutional participation structure, crypto assets play more of a "liquidity relief valve" role than a safe-haven tool in extreme risk events. When traditional markets are closed, the cryptocurrency market becomes the only operational channel for global funds to release risk, and its price behavior reflects liquidity structure more than fundamental logic. The impact of the shock extends far beyond the price level. Within hours of the strike, the Iranian leadership announced the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, one of the most vital arteries in global oil shipping, through which nearly 20% of the world's maritime oil trade passes. This massive tail risk superimposes the energy supply shock onto already high global inflation and directly impacts European and Asian economies heavily reliant on imported solar power. Even if a ceasefire is reached through negotiations within days, the pricing of tail risks is not completely eliminated. If the conflict escalates again, the market reaction could be far greater than the initial shock. For the crypto market, this shock carries two pressures: first, rising oil prices push up inflation expectations, further compressing the Fed's room for interest rate cuts; second, investors' pre-market anxieties can only be released through the crypto market. Meanwhile, the uncertain future situation in Iran is also a core macroeconomic variable for geopolitical risk signals in the second quarter. If the conflict escalates further under the political logic of a midterm election year, the suppression of risk assets will not be a repeat of the first quarter. The Fed: The fluctuating path of interest rate cuts and the delay in the liquidity inflection point. Macroeconomically, BTC, as a peripheral market in the financial industry, has always had a significant correlation with the Fed's interest rate policy.

Federal Reserve Interest Rates vs BTC Price (2022–2026)

Since 2022, there have been approximately four distinct cycles.

2022-2023: Interest Rate Hike Cycle → BTC Under Pressure

The Federal Reserve began aggressively raising interest rates at the beginning of 2022, from near zero to 5.5%. During the same period, BTC plummeted from approximately $47K to $16K. High interest rates increased the opportunity cost of holding a non-yielding, risky asset like BTC, leading to capital outflows.

Second half of 2023 - early 2024: Pause in interest rate hikes → BTC bottoms out and rebounds

While interest rates remained in the 5.25%-5.50% range, the market began pricing in expectations of interest rate cuts. The price of BTC rebounded from $16K to over $60K. Meanwhile, in January 2024, the SEC approved 11 spot BTC ETFs, marking BTC's official entry into the mainstream financial system.

September 2024 - October 2025: Interest rate cut cycle → BTC surge

The Federal Reserve cut interest rates for the first time by 50 basis points in September 2024, followed by three consecutive rate cuts, totaling 100 basis points within the year.

Three interest rate cuts were completed in 2025, totaling 1.75%. During this period, BTC surged from $66K to an all-time high of $128,198. The Federal Reserve maintained interest rates at 3.50%-3.75% at its January and March 2026 meetings, citing rising oil prices pushing up inflation expectations. Both PCE and core PCE forecasts were revised upwards to 2.7%. Coupled with tensions in the Middle East, BTC fell from $126K to approximately $74K currently. Interest rate cuts reduce the opportunity cost of holding BTC while increasing market liquidity, historically strongly correlated with BTC price increases. The Fed's dot plot indicates an expected rate cut of one time in 2026, which is not good news for the price of BTC. The Fed's policy path underwent a gradual hawkish shift in the first quarter, with rate cut expectations gradually compressed from two at the beginning of the year to near zero. The FOMC meeting on January 28th maintained the federal funds rate at 3.5%–3.75%, marking the first pause after three consecutive rate cuts in 2025. In his post-meeting statement, Powell reiterated that "more data is needed to confirm a sustained decline in inflation," a cautious but relatively neutral tone. However, the meeting minutes released on February 18 revealed a much deeper internal division than stated in the statement. The minutes showed that several officials explicitly mentioned the possibility of an interest rate hike, the first time such wording appeared in the minutes since 2023, and market confidence in interest rate cuts began to crack. On January 30, Trump announced the nomination of former Federal Reserve Governor Kevin Warsh to succeed Powell as chairman (the nomination was formally submitted to the Senate on March 4), with an expected inauguration in May. Warsh served as a Federal Reserve Governor from 2006 to 2011, and was one of the youngest and most hawkish members of the committee, having resigned in opposition to a second round of quantitative easing. It's worth noting that his public statements have softened somewhat in recent years, arguing that AI-driven productivity gains have a structural deflationary effect, thus supporting lower interest rates. However, his most prominent label remains "balance sheet reduction over interest rate cuts." Warsh has repeatedly criticized the Federal Reserve for maintaining an excessively large balance sheet, blurring the boundaries between monetary and fiscal policy, advocating for a significant reduction in the balance sheet before discussing interest rate cuts; even if nominal interest rates decline slightly, the liquidity tightening resulting from aggressive balance sheet reduction could offset the effect of interest rate cuts (some analysts estimate that every $1 trillion reduction in the balance sheet is roughly equivalent to a 50bp interest rate hike). The market's interpretation of his nomination is generally hawkish, with some institutions viewing it as "an intriguing choice for a president who wants low interest rates." Following the nomination announcement, precious metals fell first (gold fell 1.9% in a single week), the dollar strengthened, and the market began to reprice what a Fed chaired by this type means. Entering February, the situation became further complicated. On February 28, the US and Israel launched a military strike against Iran (see Section 1.2 for details), causing oil prices to jump above $100 per barrel, completely altering the inflationary landscape. Cost-based inflation driven by tariffs had not yet been digested, and the supply-side shock to energy prices compounded the situation, creating double inflationary pressure. The policy space for interest rate cuts was severely compressed, and the market began to seriously discuss a previously absurd possibility: the next step would not be a rate cut, but a rate hike. The FOMC meeting on March 18 put all of this into writing. The meeting maintained the interest rate unchanged by a vote of 11:1 (the January meeting was 10:2, with Miran and Waller opposing; in March, Waller switched to favor, with only Miran opposing – a shift itself a noteworthy signal of hawkish restraint). The post-meeting statement specifically added the phrase "the impact of developments in the Middle East on the US economy remains uncertain," formally introducing the war factor into the Fed's decision-making framework. The updated Summary of Economic Projections (SEP) and dot plot released clear hawkish signals: the 2026 inflation forecast was revised upward to 2.7%, and the median rate cut in the dot plot remained unchanged at one this year (consistent with December, Powell explicitly stated that "the median has not changed"), but 14 of the 19 committee members expect only 0–1 rate cuts this year, a clear hawkish shift in distribution; CME FedWatch shows the market has compressed its expectations for rate cuts this year from two at the beginning of the year to a maximum of one, with the possibility of zero rate cuts significantly increasing. The unemployment rate forecast remained at 4.4%, and GDP growth was slightly revised upward, showing an overall nascent form of stagflation characterized by resilient growth and sticky inflation. Bitcoin responded by falling from $74,000 to $70,900, with ETFs experiencing a net outflow of $129 million that day. Some market analysts pointed out that Warsh's first move after taking office might not be a rate cut, but a rate hike. Looking back at the first quarter, the collapse of interest rate cut expectations was not an event, but a process. From the interest rate hike discussions emerging in the January minutes, to the hawkish personnel expectations brought about by Warsh's nomination, to the second wave of inflation caused by the Iran war, and then to the March dot plot compressing the year's interest rate cuts to one, the market's initial expectation of two rate cuts was stripped away layer by layer, and the narrative of "Higher for Longer" once again took the lead. Previously, the market generally believed that "the interest rate inflection point has been established, and the liquidity inflection point is only a matter of time," but the evolution in the first quarter gave a more extreme answer. Not only is the liquidity inflection point still far away, but the interest rate inflection point itself is also at risk of being overturned. If Warsh's proposed combination of interest rate cuts and balance sheet reduction is implemented, it means that even if nominal interest rates decline, the actual liquidity environment may be tighter, and the suppressive effect on risk assets should not be underestimated. Against this macroeconomic backdrop, global risk assets are under pressure across the board: the S&P 500 has fallen more than 5% year-to-date, and the Nasdaq has fallen even more; gold ETFs have absorbed more than $16 billion in funds; Bitcoin ETFs saw a net outflow of $3.8 billion in February alone. The signal of funds migrating from digital gold to physical gold is extremely clear. ArkStream Capital will continue to monitor the evolution of interest rate cut expectations and geopolitical situations, as these are the core variables for judging the timing of subsequent liquidity inflection points. Bitcoin and Fund Structure: Narrative Falsification, ETF Differentiation and Institutionalization Process Digital Gold Stress Test One of the most noteworthy structural changes in the first quarter was the significantly enhanced correlation between Bitcoin and the Nasdaq index during stress periods. In market stress tests, Bitcoin not only failed to provide a hedging function, but also showed a downward trend almost synchronously with technology stocks. From a behavioral finance perspective, this is not surprising. Since the approval of ETFs, institutional investors have included Bitcoin in the same risk budget framework as technology growth stocks. When macro risk appetite contracts, deleveraging and reducing positions are systemic and not differentiated based on asset class. This surge in correlation marks a new stage in Bitcoin's institutionalization process: it is evolving from a standalone alternative asset into a high-beta subset of global risk asset portfolios. The first quarter data thoroughly exposed the limitations of the digital gold narrative: gold exhibited two distinct trends in the first quarter: it reached a historical high of approximately $5,280 per ounce in early January, but with rising expectations of a hawkish Fed and increasing real interest rates, a significant pullback occurred in the latter half of Q1, with a maximum drawdown of nearly 20%; while Bitcoin fell by more than 20% throughout the quarter; between November 2025 and January 2026, the Bitcoin ETF... The cumulative net outflow reached approximately $6.18 billion, marking the longest continuous outflow period in history, while gold ETFs recorded a net inflow of over $16 billion during the same period. The signal of funds migrating from digital gold to physical gold is extremely clear. The digital gold narrative surrounding BTC has become ineffective. Through data comparison, we found that BTC shows a high correlation with Nasdaq, but a very low correlation with gold.
